name: AJ Dybantsa
scouting_text: The Washington Wizards may see AJ Dybantsa as the can't-lose option with the fewest holes, while some remain worried about Darryn Peterson's bizarre season or Cameron Boozer's athletic/defensive limitations. The Wizards will presumably listen to trade calls, particularly if they prefer Peterson or Boozer and know a rival team wants Dybantsa.

name: Darryn Peterson
scouting_text: If AJ Dybantsa goes No. 1, the Utah Jazz will presumably decide between Darryn Peterson and Cameron Boozer, whose father, Carlos, works in the team's front office. On paper, Peterson looks like a cleaner fit after Utah acquired Jaren Jackson Jr., although at No. 2, CEO Danny Ainge will evaluate both players in a vacuum while looking to draft the bigger star.

name: Cameron Boozer
scouting_text: Cameron Boozer left the NBA combine without any new concerns arising over his size or athletic traits. He measured almost identically to Al Horford and tested middle of the pack, as expected. Unless the Memphis Grizzlies are enamored with the idea of replacing Ja Morant with Darius Acuff Jr., they'll be picking between Boozer and Caleb Wilson.

name: Caleb Wilson
scouting_text: Caleb Wilson should look like a strong best-player-available candidate and easy plug-and-play forward for the Chicago Bulls at the 4. There are even NBA front office members who actually prefer Wilson to Cameron Boozer, whose athletic limitations have raised some skepticism. Certain scouts sound willing to bet on Wilson's open-floor ball-handling, mid-range scoring and passing continuing to improve to complement the quickness, explosion and defensive range that Boozer lacks.

name: Keaton Wagler
scouting_text: The Los Angeles Clippers are expected to be involved in trade talks leading up to the draft, with rival teams thinking they could try to move. Keaton Wagler is right now earning looks in the No. 5-9 range with his crafty creation, special shotmaking, high IQ and adaptable game.

name: Darius Acuff Jr.
scouting_text: With the No. 6 pick, you can bet on the Brooklyn Nets trying to trade into the top four. Regardless, they'll be thinking best player available over needs wherever they pick. They'll most likely be looking at the top guards, with Darius Acuff Jr. the type of engine who'll look attractive to a rebuilding team. Per sources, he's already conducted a workout for Brooklyn.

name: Kingston Flemings
scouting_text: The Sacramento Kings figure to be hunting down a new point guard with a number of lottery-caliber ball-handlers to choose from. Kingston Flemings' measurements were disappointing, as he came in just a half-inch taller than Darius Acuff (who'd been considered undersized) with four fewer inches of length. But he surprised by leading the NBA combine in the three-point star drill (overtaking Alex Karaban), making 19-of-25 shots. He also hit 15-of-25 spot-ups threes, encouraging signs for a guard whose bread and butter in college was separating into mid-range jumpers.

name: Mikel Brown Jr.
scouting_text: Mikel Brown Jr. will be in play for teams in the No. 5-9 range. The Atlanta Hawks should have an interest in one of the draft's top guards with CJ McCollum turning 35 years old before the season. Teams have wondered how much Brown's back problems influenced the inconsistency at Louisville. He was regarded as the top guard pre-season after Darryn Peterson, and his creativity, shotmaking and playmaking remain enticing entering the draft.

name: Brayden Burries
scouting_text: Brayden Burries is being looked at as high as No. 6 by the Brooklyn Nets. He isn't expected to fall outside the top 10, with nearly all scouts confident in his strong physical profile, three-level scoring and low-maintenance style. In the late-lottery, he'll enter the best-player-available conversation for his downhill attacking, shotmaking, strong defensive tools and knack for scoring without needing heavy usage.

name: Nate Ament
scouting_text: Nate Ament has been selective about who he's working out for, but he has already visited multiple top-10 teams. He measured well in Chicago, coming in even taller than expected barefoot (6'9.5"). This is an enticing height for a player with his ability to attack and shoot from different spots. He plays well both on and off the ball.

name: Karim Lopez
scouting_text: Age, NBL production, a 6'8", 221-pound frame and expanding versatility could suggest that Karim Lopez can offer effective, immediate minutes He would be a good fit for teams that have available roles and see a valuable archetype with Lopez's potential to provide physicality at the 4, spot-up shooting and ball-screen playmaking.

name: Aday Mara
scouting_text: After measuring 7'3" barefoot with a 9'9" standing reach, Aday Mara has started to look unique with nearly unmatchable advantage-creating physical traits. The numbers at Michigan backed up that idea—Mara finished fourth in the nation in box plus-minus with a rare combination of 12.0 block percentage and 19.0 assist percentage. Mara separates himself from the other unicorns his size with high-level passing to complement the rim protection, high-percentage finishing and sound footwork.

name: Yaxel Lendeborg
scouting_text: NBA teams won't put much stock into Yaxel Lendeborg's NBA combine performance or workouts. There is enough tape of his versatility and gradual improvement as a shooter and defender, and there is clearly enough evidence of high-impact minutes after his national championship run at Michigan.

name: Labaron Philon Jr.
scouting_text: Teams considering Labaron Philon will be drawn to his creativity. It's also difficult to find any worrisome holes in his statistical profile after he improved his shooting and finishing. He could slip to late lottery behind the young guards who may look more like natural playmakers. But unless Philon's slower release affects his three-point shot, there's a high likelihood his scoring ability will translate.

name: Cameron Carr
scouting_text: A projected first-round pick entering the combine, Cameron Carr bet on himself by choosing to scrimmage and likely earned himself more money. His 30 points further strengthened his projection as a surefire NBA scoring wing with translatable shotmaking firepower and athleticism. He was able to separate with ease over defenders and convert, both as a shooter and finisher. The extreme, high likelihood of a three-and-D floor outcome should put Carr in play at the back end of the lottery.

name: Ebuka Okorie
scouting_text: Ebuka Okorie is drawing interest from late-lottery teams with workouts for the Atlanta Hawks, Dallas Mavericks and Chicago Bulls. Measuring at 6'1.25", 186 pounds with a 6'7.75" wingspan was considered a win for the freshman. He avoided red flags about his size, which scouts were somewhat worried about entering the combine.

name: Morez Johnson Jr.
scouting_text: Morez Johnson could have only helped himself at the NBA combine. After measuring 6'9" barefoot, 250 pounds with a 7'3.5" wingspan, he surprised with 17-of-25 makes in the three-point star drill, got up for a 39.5" max vertical and one of the top lane agility times. NBA teams understand his offensive limitations, but they also value what he does well and believe that physicality, finishing, defensive versatility and motor are sure to translate.

name: Hannes Steinbach
scouting_text: Hannes Steinbach measured like an NBA center at 6'10¼" barefoot with a 7'2" wingspan. The question is how many teams will be confident playing him at the 5 without great shot-blocking or daunting rim protection. Offensively, he's too skilled to overthink in the mid-to-late first round. Unteachable instincts help maximize his advanced footwork and soft hands on post-ups and putbacks.

name: Christian Anderson
scouting_text: The vibes around Christian Anderson are strong coming out of the combine, where he measured a 6'6.25" wingspan, got up for a 40.5" max vertical and lit up a number of shooting drills. He's the more natural playmaker compared to Labaron Philon, Bennett Stirtz and Ebuka Okorie, and he currently has a strong case as the best shooter in that group.

name: Allen Graves
scouting_text: Scouts are anticipating first-round looks for Allen Graves based on his feel, analytics and specific versatility. He was never going to perform well at the NBA combine, as his athletic limitations are well-documented and mostly accepted. Graves will earn consideration from teams that see value in a strong power forward who shoots threes, thrives under the offensive glass, disrupts defensively and lights up analytic models.

name: Bennett Stirtz
scouting_text: The needle won't move much for Bennett Stirtz during the predraft process. He proved himself by nearly matching his big numbers at Drake with the same production and an Elite 8 run at Iowa. He's not going to win any athletic or physical competitions, and being a 23-year-old rookie will likely dissuade lottery teams in a draft that's loaded with ball-handlers. But in the teens or 20s, Stirtz will look like a value pick whose college impact, shotmaking, IQ and finishing/touch simply looks too advanced to write off because of age or limited speed/explosion.

name: Chris Cenac Jr.
scouting_text: Despite flaws in Chris Cenac's statistical profile, there will be teams willing to bet on a 19-year-old with his 6'10" (barefoot) size, 7'5" wingspan, 240-pound frame, shooting confidence and motor. He'll be a popular reach candidate for teams looking to fill gaps and aren't concerned with finding high-upside scorers.

name: Jayden Quaintance
scouting_text: Jayden Quaintance's draft stock will be heavily tied to medical reports after last year's ACL tear and this year's lack of availability. He measured exceptionally well in Chicago with a 7'5" wingspan. And there is enough tape dating back to high school to buy a special defensive prospect.

name: Meleek Thomas
scouting_text: Shooting versatility and the ability to play in ball screens with pacing, a dangerous pull-up game and floater should help Meleek Thomas succeed in a combo-guard role. He mostly defended well at Arkansas and managed to blend in nicely despite having to share time with Darius Acuff Jr. Measuring 6'3" barefoot does hurt his projection, however, and not being able to show that much playmaking will force teams to use their imaginations more than they'd want.

name: Dailyn Swain
scouting_text: There's been a brighter spotlight on Dailyn Swain, particularly after Texas won three NCAA tournament games. His NBA role will likely differ from the one he plays now, where he's often handling the ball in ball screen and transition situations. But he'll certainly be able to use the skills he's developed to become a more well-rounded Swiss Army knife, specifically his improved creation, pull-up game, floater and passing.

name: Luigi Suigo
scouting_text: Luigi Suigo had the NBA combine gym talking after he measured just under 7'3" barefoot, 289 pounds with 7'5" wingspan and a ridiculous 9'6" reach. That size combined with his 18 three-point makes and 15.0 offensive rebounding percentage for Mega will look very enticing in the back of the first round, should he choose to stay in the draft.

name: Koa Peat
scouting_text: Koa Peat staying in the draft indicates either confidence in draft stock or his chance to develop more effectively in the pros. While questions about shooting and fit have reduced interest around the freshman, they may have also helped turn Peat into a buy-low value pick for teams interested in adding a physical, interior scorer and frontcourt passing asset.

name: Isaiah Evans
scouting_text: Isaiah Evans will be for teams who have a specific need for more shooting and floor-spacing. His limitations as a creator are known, but Evans can look very useful to teams that need off-ball scorers and shotmakers. The improvements he's made attacking in straight lines and exploding above the rim has also added an extra layer of useful offense to his game.

name: Sergio de Larrea
scouting_text: Sergio de Larrea is currently producing for Valencia in the EuroLeague playoffs, which is why he couldn't attend the combine. He's now put together consecutive years of accurate three-point shooting and strong playmaking rates. De Larrea is sure to draw first-round consideration, particularly if more college players go back to school before the May 27 deadline.

name: Henri Veesaar
scouting_text: Henri Veesaar became one of the draft's top play-finishers who also hit 40 three-pointers. He doesn't block many shots for a 7-footer, but offensively, he has become incredibly productive and efficient with a translatable and coveted inside-out scoring package.

name: Zuby Ejiofor
scouting_text: Zuby Ejiofor will earn looks from teams that value his energy, physicality and defensive activity. But he's also flashed enough shotmaking, face-up drives and post moves to potentially provide bonus offense at the next level.

name: Tarris Reed Jr.
scouting_text: Tarris Reed Jr. put together a rare statistical season, posting a 9.0 block percentage, 13.0 offensive rebounding percentage and 15.0 assist percentage. His combination of strength, paint touch, passing and rim protection should put him in first-round conversations for teams that want bigs.

name: Joshua Jefferson
scouting_text: Improved shooting and a new playmaking role have turned Joshua Jefferson into a complete player and believable NBA prospect. Aside from the improved three-point efficiency, his passing has really popped the most, particularly for a 6'9", 240-pound forward. But he's tough around the basket with strength and touch, and he's quick and smart defensively. There will be NBA teams that would rather target youth and upside, but Jefferson has turned himself into a popular name with so much versatility and adaptability.

name: Alex Karaban
scouting_text: NBA teams know Alex Karaban's strengths, limitations and potential pro role after 151 college games and three Final Fours. But to see him improve athletically, which this year's combine tests show after weak results in 2024, has been deemed very encouraging. He could earn late first-round looks from teams that see an NBA-ready shooter, cutter and professional.

name: Baba Miller
scouting_text: Baba Miller is trending upward after measuring almost 6'11" barefoot, testing well during foot speed drills and going for 20 points during Wednesday's NBA combine scrimmage. Even if shooting remains a problem, his ability to finish/rebound like a big and handle/pass like a wing remains intriguing.

name: Trevon Brazile
scouting_text: Trevon Brazile suddenly looks like a more believable pro prospect with improved shooting and driving to complement the size and athleticism at the rim. In the second round, a team should see a bet worth making on his three-ball to reach a league-average level. He has an easy-to-project archetype and role as a stretch-4/finisher and threat to attack closeouts.

name: Jack Kayil
scouting_text: Jack Kayil couldn't make it to the combine with his season still going in Germany. He earned an invite and spot on boards with shotmaking (1.7 3PTM) and an impressive 30.6 assist percentage. The fact that Kayil is staying in the draft suggests he's confident in his projected range based on feedback.

name: Braden Smith
scouting_text: Despite obvious physical limitations, scouts still sound optimistic about Braden Smith's ability to carve out a setup role. He got to any spot he wanted to in NBA combine scrimmages, and that IQ and craftiness for creating for others could be useful to a number of teams.

name: Ugonna Onyenso
scouting_text: In the 40s or 50s, the potential reward tied to Ugonna Onyenso's 7'5" wingspan and 17.4 block percentage can outweigh his offensive limitations. Virginia unleashed him in the ACC tournament, when he swatted 21 shots in just three games, including nine against Duke and Cameron Boozer.

name: Izaiyah Nelson
scouting_text: So far during the predraft process, Izaiyah Nelson has made some head-turning athletic, mobility and energy plays at the Portsmouth Invitational and the NBA combine. He also measured 6'9" with a 7'3" wingspan, exciting numbers for a potential specialty big who can provide value with play-finishing and defensive playmaking.

name: Richie Saunders
scouting_text: A torn ACL ended Richie Saunders' career at BYU. From a draft-stock perspective, there wasn't much left for him to sell teams on. The injury shouldn't factor into the equation, with the assumption he wasn't expected to play many NBA minutes next year, anyway. He'll be an option for a team that sees a shooting specialist and finds value in the second round.

name: Nick Martinelli
scouting_text: Seniors and players like Nick Martinelli could benefit from NIL bringing underclassmen back to college. Despite limited athletic ability and an unorthodox game, he was a top-10 scorer and improved shooter (41.7 percent 3PT) at 6'7". Martinelli's scoring versatility should be worth looking into, and after two productive NBA combine scrimmages, he should have a good chance to crack top-60 boards.

name: Otega Oweh
scouting_text: He carried Kentucky to its first NCAA tournament win with 35 points against Santa Clara. Playing a larger role that allowed for more on-ball reps and shots, he wound up with career highs of 45 threes and 2.7 assists. Though he's missing a specialty skill, Oweh's on/off-ball versatility and defense could draw second-round interest.

name: Jaden Bradley
scouting_text: Shooting has always kept interest from building around Jaden Bradley, but as the point guard for a No. 1 seed, he's done too good of a job running offense and applying pressure at both ends. NBA teams could see an interesting two-way playmaker who can penetrate, make good decisions and cause problems defensively.

name: Emanuel Sharp
scouting_text: Emanuel Sharp's 309 career threes should have NBA teams considering him for a shooting specialist role. He looked equally as potent during NBA combine scrimmages as he did through his time at Houston.

name: Ryan Conwell
scouting_text: Measuring 6'2" could hurt the stock of a non-playmaking guard. He'll remain in the second-round mix for teams looking to add more off-ball scoring and shooting.

name: Ja'Kobi Gillespie
scouting_text: Despite measuring under 6'0" barefoot, Ja'Kobi Gillespie's 28 points during his first combine scrimmage highlighted enticing microwave shotmaking ability. Between his perimeter scoring firepower and playmaking at Tennessee, teams could picture an instant-offense guard to bring off the bench.

name: Aaron Nkrumah
scouting_text: Aaron Nkrumah has capitalized on the draft process, having impressed at the G League Camp and earned an invite to the NBA combine. He looked like he belonged there during scrimmages, and given the archetype his frame, improved shooting, 3.0 assists and 2.8 steals can create, there should be enough second-round interest in Nkrumah after he presumably goes through over a dozen workouts.

name: Tobi Lawal
scouting_text: The combination of 6'7" size and near record-setting bounce could be enough for a team to draft Tobi Lawal in the second round. His ability to make athletic finishes and plays that others can't has become worth looking into for a specialty role.

name: Dillon Mitchell
scouting_text: It may take a specific general manager and roster to see value in Dillon Mitchell's game, but he sold it very well on Wednesday. Athleticism for finishing, strong passing instincts, defensive tools and motor create glue-guy potential worth looking at in the second round.

name: Milos Uzan
scouting_text: Milos Uzan hasn't shot well from three this year, but there is enough evidence of shotmaking to look past this season's percentages. His float game, passing IQ and backcourt versatility should have scouts willing to gamble on the possibility that Uzan's jumper will be better than the numbers suggest.

name: Kylan Boswell
scouting_text: Kylan Boswell still doesn't look like a reliable shooter, but he's been threatening enough for a guard who shoots 61.0 percent inside the arc, makes smart decisions and adds a sense of leadership. He's been one of the nation's top-graded pick-and-roll ball-handlers throughout the season.

name: Wyatt Fricks
scouting_text: Wyatt Fricks is earning workouts after making 51 threes and blocking 48 shots. He's an interesting late second-round option for teams interested in adding a stretch big who keeps improving his shooting.

name: Duke Miles
scouting_text: Duke Miles should be on teams' second-round radar after a year of improved pull-up shooting and playmaking. He'll earn a chance at the next level with his defensive pressure, but he's become a more well-rounded offensive weapon creating in ball-screen situations and making jumpers off the catch and dribble.

name: Felix Okpara
scouting_text: At 237 pounds with a 9'4" reach, Felix Okpara's body type and measurements alone should be worth drafting in the second round. He had some strong moments at the NBA combine showcasing his finishing and shot-blocking tools.

name: Maliq Brown
scouting_text: The nation's leader defensive box plus-minus, Maliq Brown was also a productive player during scrimmages at the NBA combine. He's limited and unorthodox offensively, but his defensive instincts, passing and finishing should earn him a top-60 spot on boards.

name: Bruce Thornton
scouting_text: The undersized label won't look as worrisome this late in the second round. Thornton has shown improvement each season to become one of the nation's most well-rounded scoring guards with on-ball creation and off-ball and off-ball shooting.

name: Rafael Castro
scouting_text: Rafael Castro's standout showing at the Portsmouth Invitational led to a G League Elite Camp, an NBA combine invite—and scouts going back to review more film from George Washington. At 6'10", his quickness and bounce translate to easy baskets off cuts, rolls and offensive boards. His 19.1 rebounding percentage, 3.7 steal percentage and 7.6 block percentage remain promising indicators for defensive upside.

name: Keyshawn Hall
scouting_text: On his fourth school in four years, Keyshawn Hall was an incredibly efficient spot-up scorer with his catch-and-shoot game and slashing. Age and poor defense hold him back, but late in the second round, a team could see a gamble worth taking on a 6'7", 240-pound wing averaging over 20 points in the SEC.

name: Trey Kaufman-Renn
scouting_text: Though not the flashiest or most versatile big, Trey Kaufman-Renn consistently produced off his soft hands, instincts and motor. He's an option for teams that see the interior scoring, passing and rebounding translating.

name: Cameron Boozer
scouting_text: At 6-foot-8 and 253 pounds, Boozer is the most polished player in the class. He scores from the post with both footwork and power, hits 40% of his 3s on high volume, and has enough handle to run offense as a point forward. He shifts between those modes based on what the defense gives him, and that adaptability led to a 35-win season at Duke and the Naismith Player of the Year. The worry is that he’s not a vertical athlete and the foundation of all that production — overpowering smaller defenders — gets diminished against NBA length. Plus, he’s a modern tweener on defense, lacking the explosiveness and size to protect the rim full-time and the lateral quickness to switch onto guards. With the bloodline of two-time All-Star Carlos Boozer, the team that drafts him is betting that skill, adaptability, and a track record of winning at every level all lead to superstardom.

name: AJ Dybantsa
scouting_text: Dybantsa could become one of the NBA’s most unstoppable shot-creators. At 6-foot-9, he has a special blend of athletic tools the way he bends, shifts, and explodes with the ball in his hands. Dybantsa led the nation with 25.5 points per game while breaking Danny Ainge’s 48-year-old BYU freshman scoring record with a 43-point eruption. He gets to the rim at will, cooks in the midrange, draws fouls at a high rate, and displays point forward potential. What will determine his upside is whether he can become a knockdown 3-point shooter, as well as a more impactful defender to take full advantage of his physical tools. But the native of Brockton, Mass., has a tremendously high floor with his scoring skill alone.

name: Darryn Peterson
scouting_text: Peterson is a buttery smooth scorer with a blend of fluid body control and positional size that gives him the ingredients to become an elite NBA player. At the high school level, he was a dynamic playmaker who used his burst to get into the teeth of defenses and generate buckets for himself and his teammates, while also showing off the kind of shot-making that draws comparisons to Hall of Famers. At Kansas, he thrived in an off-ball role, stroking jumpers out of movement actions and showing he can scale up or down depending on what a roster needs. Even when he isn't scoring, he's a high-impact defender who causes chaos off-ball and has the 6-foot-11 wingspan to switch screens. The concern isn't his game. It's his body. He missed 11 of 35 games and pulled himself out of others due to cramping, capping off one of the weirdest freshman seasons in recent memory. Questions about his burst, his availability, and what exactly is going on under the hood are going to define how NBA front offices feel about him at the top of this draft.

name: Caleb Wilson
scouting_text: Wilson is the most gifted athlete in the draft class. He's 6-foot-9 with springs for legs. When he's flying above the rim, finishing through contact, and chasing down every shot in his area code, he looks like a future franchise cornerstone. But the conversation changes when you watch his jumper because he hasn’t shown any consistency as a shooter at any level. Still, even without the jumper, he has star upside.

name: Mikel Brown Jr.
scouting_text: When Brown is in the zone, he has an unstoppable pull-up jumper, an ambidextrous finishing ability, and the quick reads to rifle passes before the defense has time to react. He had a 45-point breakout performance in February after a back injury dogged him all freshman year and then ended his year later in the month. The absences muddy the evaluation and leave real questions about his consistency that may not get answered until he’s fully healthy.

name: Keaton Wagler
scouting_text: Wagler showed up at Illinois as a four-star recruit with no expectations of becoming a one-and-done. But he quickly became the orchestrator of a high-powered Illinois offense with his high-IQ playmaking and crafty scoring. Then he scored 46 at Purdue against a top-ranked team in the country — the most points by any freshman in Big Ten history. Then he kept rolling, and led the team to an unexpected Final Four appearance. Now he’s a lottery lock, but he’s a quirky player in that he logged zero dunks. To become an NBA star, Wagler needs to overcome a lack of traditional athleticism. But the best case is that his feel for the game is enough for him to continue ascending.

name: Darius Acuff Jr.
scouting_text: Acuff is not the biggest guard or the most explosive athlete, but he reads defenses like someone who's been in the league for a decade. He emerged as a freshman as a skilled, low-turnover playmaker. And that’s not even what he’s best at. Acuff is a wiry scorer who can get a bucket from anywhere on the floor with a quick trigger, slippery handle, and a feel for manipulating defenses. He has a knack for clutch moments too. The question that follows every undersized guard into the draft is whether the brilliance survives contact with bigger, longer, faster defenders.

name: Brayden Burries
scouting_text: Burries arrived at Arizona as a top-10 recruit, started slow, and then erupted once conference play began, helping lead his team to the Final Four. He's a physical, versatile scorer who can beat you from all three levels, rebounds like a forward, and competes hard on defense. But he's a methodical creator rather than an explosive one, and his shooting history before Arizona gives scouts reason to wonder whether the efficiency is real or a blip.

name: Aday Mara
scouting_text: Mara stepped on UCLA’s campus as a lottery-projected center from Spain. Then he fell off draft boards during two forgettable seasons there before transferring to Michigan and becoming one of the best true 5s in the country on his way to winning the national championship. He reads the floor like a guard, finishes with both hands, and swats shots with elite timing. The complication is he doesn't shoot from outside, makes below 60% of his free throws, and opponents are going to attack him on the perimeter.

name: Nate Ament
scouting_text: Players who can handle, shoot off the dribble, and stand at 6-foot-10 don’t grow on trees. This physical foundation kept Ament in lottery consideration even after a dreadful start to his freshman season when he struggled to score efficiently and make an impact defensively. But over the second half of the year for Tennessee, he flipped a switch and shots began to fall. He averaged 23.8 points over a six-game stretch in January and February that reminded everyone why he was a top recruit in the country. Then he dealt with an ankle injury that ruined his momentum entering March and he severely struggled during the tournament.

name: AJ Dybantsa
scouting_text: – AJ stays at no.1 both for his upside and his seemingly high floor. He’s almost guaranteed to score the ball in bunches at the NBA level and has the type of competitive fire you want from your franchise cornerstone. There are areas of his game that need improvement but he already brings so much to the table that I feel comfortable having him at the top of this loaded draft class.

name: Darryn Peterson
scouting_text: – The enigma that is Darryn Peterson comes with loads of scoring talent and a nice all around game checkered with a few orange flags. If he hits he’ll be the best player from this class but his one year at Kansas raised enough questions in my mind that he falls to no.2 on my big board.

name: Caleb Wilson
scouting_text: – I have to trust my eyes when it comes to evaluating Caleb Wilson. I get that its unusual to have him over Boozer but I’m so enamored with Wilson’s athletic traits, production and upside that I can’t have him lower than this. I first moved Wilson to the no.3 spot back

name: Cameron Boozer
scouting_text: If Boozer falls to no.4 a team will be getting awesome value and a future cornerstone of their franchise. While I don’t see the same level of upside that some see with him, I still think Boozer will be a bona fide All Star and be really good for a really long time. The top of this draft is loaded and all of the top 4 guys are likely to be stars.

name: Kingston Flemings
scouting_text: – With this ranking it’s more about projection than a finished product. Flemings has unique athletic traits and the type of explosive first step and above the rim ability that great NBA guards have. He shot the ball better than expected this season for Houston but still has room for growth with his shooting form and consistency. If he can really be a knock-down shooter he’s going to be special.

name: Darius Acuff Jr.
scouting_text: – Acuff Jr. has a level of polish to his game that you don’t often see from young guards coming into the league. He’s not the biggest guy and not an above the rim athlete but he’s got a special ability to put the ball in the basket and make big shots in big moments. It’s going to be really fun to see where he lands, to see if he gets the keys to a NBA offense and to watch how his game translates to the NBA.

name: Mikel Brown Jr.
scouting_text: – I’m tempted to have Brown in Tier 2. I think he can be a nuclear scorer at the next level and could end up as one of the better 3pt shooters in the league before too long. Add in his explosive athleticism and good feel for finding teammates and he’s close to being an elite prospect.

name: Keaton Wagler
scouting_text: . – Wagler’s meteoric rise up draft boards culminates with him being a top 10 player on many lists and a sure-fire lottery pick. I love his feel and his uncanny ability to get to his spots. NBA size and athleticism will be an adjustment for him (as it is for everyone) but I think he can continue to be effective with his herky-jerky style of play.

name: Brayden Burries
scouting_text: Burries is a lethal scoring guard who just seems like a cold-blooded assassin when it comes to putting the ball in the basket. He’s a guy who can play on or off the ball and should be seamless fit with any NBA team.

name: Hannes Steinbach
scouting_text: – Hannes has skills that should translate immediately with his rebounding, play-finishing and ability to get up and down the floor in a hurry. He might not be the most exciting player in this range of the draft but I think he’ll return good value.

name: Aday Mara
scouting_text: – Mara has some boom/bust potential but at this range of the board I think it makes sens to take the swing on him. If he can be a defensive anchor and be a partial offensive hub he’ll be a hugely valuable player in the NBA. There’s a chance the speed of the game and the hunting of mismatches could be tough for him to handle but again, it’s worth a shot in the late lottery range.

name: Labaron Philon
scouting_text: – Philon is the type of productive player that can be overlooked because he’s not the biggest guy, isn’t a great above the rim player and isn’t a super young freshman. This could lead to a NBA team getting a steal outside of the top 10. He’s made massive improvements over the past two season and there’s every reason to believe he’ll continue on that trajectory.

name: Karim Lopez
scouting_text: – Playing in the NBL has lead to Lopez being a bit overlooked but I think he’s got some sneaky star potential and could be ranked much higher when we look back on this draft in a few years. He’s got awesome strength for a young forward and does a great job of handling contact and imposing his will on the game. He’s still a teenager and it’s scary to imagine how strong and physical he’ll be in a few years. I’m intrigued.

name: Morez Johnson Jr.
scouting_text: – Every NBA team can use a player like Morez. He’s a powerful, high-energy enforcer who has a big impact in the paint, changes ends well and can anchor a defense, despite not being the biggest center out there. He impacts the game in many ways without needing the ball in his hands and is just a rock solid guy who contributes to winning.

name: Yaxel Lendeborg
scouting_text: – The history of players his age becoming stars is grim. Yaxel is ancient by draft standards and so theoretically his upside is limited but the hope is that he still has more aress of his game to develop and that he’s just a late bloomer. He does promise to be very NBA ready and should step right in and play meaningful minutes for whichever team drafts him.

name: Chris Cenac Jr.
scouting_text: – Cenac Jr. is loaded with upside and really came on strong over the course of the season. He’s got the size and athleticism to hold his own in the league and his ability to space the floor gives him starter upside. He might not be the biggest contributor in year one but his upside is worth the swing in the late lottery or mid first round.

name: Cameron Carr
scouting_text: – A long and wiry athletic wing who can really shoot the 3-ball and play above the rim, yes please. Carr is an off ball player who might not be a star at the next level but should be a valuable piece who plays a complimentary role.

name: Nate Ament
scouting_text: Ament is a special talent with unique abilities for a player his size but also comes with some major question marks around his lack of separation ability and his overall lack of burst. I think he’s a fine value at this point of the draft but I’d still have some hesitation with taking him over more NBA-ready/proven players.

name: Allen Graves
scouting_text: – A do-it-all forward who came out of nowhere to be a potential first rounder. I’d love to see him in the transfer portal instead of the draft but after such a good season for the Broncos he could be NBA-bound.

name: Dailyn Swain
scouting_text: – Swain has been a steady riser all season long and is now in the late lottery range on my big board. He’s a NBA level athlete with all the size and talent to be a high level NBA player. I like how his outside shot has developed over the years and how he’s improved his dribble-drive game. He appears to have plenty of more room for improvement and is just starting to put it all together on the court.

name: Tarris Reed Jr.
scouting_text: – A March Madness hero, Reed Jr. has done as much as any player in the country to lift his draft stock and will now be getting first round looks. He’s a great rebounder on both ends, a skill that should translate well for him at the NBA level. He doesn’t need to be a high volume offensive player to be effective and looks the part of a starting big, or first big off the bench, which is solid value in the 20’s or 30’s.

name: Ebuka Okorie
scouting_text: – Okorie has been an absolute stud this year, coming in as an under-recruited guy and scorching opponents to the tune of 21 points per game while looking to be in total control of the offense.

name: Henri Veesaar
scouting_text: – Veesaar is unlikely to be a star in the NBA but at this point of the draft board finding a really solid starting level center who can stretch the floor would be a really good value. He’s a high feel player who makes really good reads and is a good big-to-big passer. I’m higher on him than most and think he’ll be really good in the NBA.

name: Bennett Stirtz
scouting_text: – I was a little too high on Stirtz to start the season but I still like him as a mid/late first rounder. He could be a nice third guard who has some versatility playing on or off the ball. Even if he’s not a starter I think he can be a valuable piece to a winning team.

name: Luigi Suigo
scouting_text: – Suigo is still open to going the college route and has some heavy interest from some blue bloods but if he stays in the draft he could be a riser on draft boards thanks to his unique size and skill. It’s not every day you find a 7-4 guy that can move, space the floor and has a solid feel for the game. He’s young and will need time to develop but you just can’t teach the tools that he’s got.

name: Koa Peat
scouting_text: – Peat had a tough NBA combine, looking like an average athlete with good strength but not great size, who can’t shoot. That’s not a great combo for a NBA player. But Peat has proven to be a winner at every level so far and I think he can be a solid rotation player in the league. That’s worth a first round pick.

name: Meleek Thomas
scouting_text: – A microwave shooter/scorer who can get hot but lacks consistency. Will need to round out his game and do more than just score the rock. Could use some more time in school and would likely spend a lot of time in the G League if he enters the draft this year.

name: Christian Anderson
scouting_text: – I think there’s a real chance Anderson out-plays this ranking. He’s not the biggest guard out there but is so skilled, is a great decision-maker no the court and has a rare combination of play-making and scoring ability. He’s also going to get a really big NIL offer so there’s a chance he returns to school if he’s projected in this range.

name: Jayden Quaintance
scouting_text: – It’s been a really tough year for JQ after ACL surgery last March and some ongoing issues with his recovery. It might make sense for him to return for another year in school to really show how good he can be. But there’s risk with that path as well. He’s a unique defensive talent but the injury history and the lack of an offensive game hurts his draft stock. He’s still incredibly young and has plenty of time to develop into a good NBA player if his body holds up.

name: Zuby Ejiofor
scouting_text: – A high energy big man who has taken the next step with his scoring and his overall skill level. Lacks ideal size but is quick, powerful and plays with an edge. I could see him being a playable guy off a NBA bench who brings energy and a change of pace up front.

name: Pavle Bačko
scouting_text: – One of the youngest prospects in the draft, the big-bodied center is a highly skilled player who gives some Nikola Jokic vibes. He’s emerged as a star in his age group over the past 12 months and could be a big riser during the draft process. It only takes one team to fall in love with him for him to be a first rounder.

name: Jaden Bradley
scouting_text: – Bradley has gotten better and better every season and now looks like a solid back-up point guard option. He’s learned to harness his athletic talents and become more of a passer and play-maker. I’m a big fan.

name: Joshua Jefferson
scouting_text: Jefferson is old, a bit slow and doesn’t really excite you with his style of play but man, he is just damn good at the game of basketball. At some point in the draft it’s less about upside and more about finding NBA players who can contribute to winning games.

name: Isaiah Evans
scouting_text: – Evans might be more of a bench scorer than a starter and so his draft stock is probably not as high as some think. He’s made really good improvements in his all-around game since last season and if he continues that trend he’ll have a long NBA career. He still needs to add strength but he’s been trending in the right direction.

name: Alex Karaban
scouting_text: – Karaban will go down as one of the greats in the UConn programs history. His NBA role is a little murky but big shot-makers have value and he’ll get a long look from NBA teams. He might not be a high upside guy but he does some things at a high level and has a chance to be a solid role player at the next level.

name: Trevon Brazile
scouting_text: – He’s got great tools and some of the flashes he shows are breathtaking. I think he still needs some developmental time before being a NBA guy but I’d swing on the upside at this point in the draft.

name: Ryan Conwell
scouting_text: A rock solid 6-3 guard who loves to let it fly from downtown. Is better in an off-ball role but can give you some minutes at point. An older player but one who could contribute early.

name: Jack Kayil
scouting_text: – Kayil is a talented combo guard who will need to shoot the ball better but has nice size and athleticism.

name: Keyshawn Hall
scouting_text: I’m a big Keyshawn Hall fan. He’s got a pro-ready frame and a really nice 3pt shot. I’d take him early to mid second round, and can also see him continuing to rise up the board.

name: Baba Miller
scouting_text: – It’s been a long road for Baba to get here but he’s been playing really good ball this year and still has that impressive combination of size and mobility. He’s shown a willingness to harness his athletic prowess and live more in the post, make plays in transition and play more like a big forward instead of a guard.

name: Ja’kobi Gillespie
scouting_text: An undersized scoring guard who is really tough to stop. Looks like a guy who could be valuable bench guard.

name: Dillon Mitchell –
scouting_text: – NBA teams love to bet on tools and Mitchell has elite tools. He’ll be a developmental player but has some long-term potential.

name: Tyler Bilodeau
scouting_text: – Tyler isn’t a great athlete and might not have great upside but he’s just a damn good basketball player who can really shoot the rock. He’s got a toughness to his game that allows him to hold his own in the paint against bigger players. But it’s his shooting ability that will get him on a NBA court.

name: Quadir Copeland
scouting_text: – Copeland plays a controlled and intentional game, gets to his spots and knows how to orchestrate an offense. I think he can make a NBA roster.

name: Otega Oweh
scouting_text: – Oweh isn’t super flashy but has two-way ability and NBA tools.

name: Fletcher Loyer
scouting_text: – He’s about as NBA-ready as they come. He’s probably not a starter in the league but I could see him having a solid NBA career and finding some success at the NBA level. He seems like the type of player a NBA team would love to add as a cheap roster option.

name: Aaron Nkrumah
scouting_text: – A wiry long-armed athlete who has been one of the stars of the pre-draft process.

name: Trey Kaufman-Renn
scouting_text: – A steady, boring player who just gets it done. He’s not going to get a fan base hyped but could end up being a valuable do-it-all forward off a NBA bench.

name: Richie Saunders
scouting_text: – A flamethrower from outside, Saunders is a natural scorer who moves well without the ball and lets it fly with a ton of confidence. He’s a much older prospect and should be ready to play some minutes early in his career…once he recovers from his recent ACL tear.

name: Braden Smith
scouting_text: – Super undersized by incredibly dynamic, Smith could be a fun guard to have coming off the bench to change the tempo.

name: Nick Martinelli
scouting_text: – A high scoring wing who is tough and plays with physicality. Will have to shoot the ball better from 3 to make it but I like the improvements he’s made and think he can get there.

name: Lamar Wilkerson
scouting_text: – A lights out shooter with good size and ball handling ability? Sign me up.

name: Melvin Council Jr. –
scouting_text: – A high volume shooter who needs to do a better job of setting up teammates and shooting with better efficiency. His ability to get to the rim and score in iso is intriguing.

name: Sergio de Larrea
scouting_text: – A slick passing big guard who doesn’t blow you away with athleticism but is crafty, shifty and can get to his spots. A bit older than other international prospects but has a lot of game.

name: Tobi Lawal
scouting_text: – In the late second round range I think Lawal is an athlete worth taking a flyer on. He’s a developmental guy but has rare physical abilities that you just can’t teach.

name: Izaiyah Nelson
scouting_text: – A big riser late in the season and during the draft process, Nelson is a really good athlete who does the big man things well and plays a solid all around game. He’s not a big name but is a nice talent who is looking like a second round pick.

name: Emanuel Sharpe
scouting_text: – It’s easy to imagine Sharpe as a bench guard who brings his mature game and NBA-readiness to a NBA team. And it’s easy to imagine him lighting up the G League for a few years first.

name: Felix Okpara
scouting_text: – A solid athlete for a near 7-footer who can hold his own in the middle and make all the big man plays. Isn’t the most powerful guy but should continue to add strength and weight.

name: Bruce Thornton
scouting_text: – He’s a baller, a gamer and a winner who looks the part of a solid back up point guard at the NBA level.

name: Bryce Hopkins
scouting_text: – A versatile forward who is a jack-of-trades type but a master of none.

name: Seth Trimble
scouting_text: – If he could shoot the ball he’d be a guaranteed NBA player but even without a jumper his athleticism could land him on a NBA roster, in time.

name: Tyler Nickel
scouting_text: – Nickel is a shooter with size and could find some spot minutes at the NBA level thanks to his ability to knockdown shots from deep.

name: Ugonna Onyenso
scouting_text: – A defensive-minded player who has good movement skills for his size. A developmental guy.

name: Milos Uzan
scouting_text: – A nice all around player who could find a role off a NBA bench.

name: Jaxon Kohler
scouting_text: – You don’t hear much about Kohler in draft circles but the dude is really talented and scores the ball at a NBA level. He’s not a great athlete but his shooting ability will give him a fighting chance.

name: Malik Reneau –
scouting_text: – A physical four-man who lacks ideal size but just gets it done. Isn’t flashy but is very productive.

name: Nick Boyd –
scouting_text: – Not the biggest or quickest guy out there but Boyd can really put the ball in the basket. And that skill could take him a long way.

name: Malique Lewis
scouting_text: – An athletic wing with good size and a developing skill-set.

name: Tobe Awaka –
scouting_text: – He doesn’t have the NBA skill-set that most 6-7 players have but he’s a tough-nosed rebounder who throws his weight around in the paint.

name: Kylan Boswell
scouting_text: – A steady veteran PG who isn’t flashy but knows how to run a team.

name: Maliq Brown
scouting_text: – An elite defender who could bring value as a situational player but really struggles on the offensive end.

name: AJ Dybantsa
scouting_text: – The Wizards win the lottery sweepstakes and will have a tough choice between a number of elite level prospects. Dybantsa would give them a big wing who can score the ball in bunches and impose his will on the game. He’s an alpha dog who can set the tone for the team for the next decade.

name: Darryn Peterson
scouting_text: – If the cramping issues are really behind him Peterson could/should be a superstar for the Jazz. He’s a rare talent with elite shooting touch and ability to get buckets whenever he wants. The Jazz have a strong young core in place and are a franchise that is heading in the right direction. There are some small orange flags with Peterson, which could cause him to slide a spot or two on draft day but the talent is certainly there.

name: Caleb Wilson
scouting_text: – Wilson is a special talent with as much upside as anyone in this draft class. The Grizzlies moved on from JJJ and will now have a chance to replace him with an even more dynamic big forward, to pair with Edey up front.

name: Cam Boozer
pro_comp: Michael Beasley, Al Horford, Kevin Love
scouting_text: – The Bulls were big winners of the lottery moving into the top four and getting a shot at Boozer, or whoever falls out of the top 3. Boozer would be a change of pace for the team as a half court player who isn’t the most fleet of foot but he’s far too talented to pass on, if he falls this far.

name: Darius Acuff Jr.
pro_comp: Stephon Marbury, Coby White, Damian Lillard
scouting_text: Acuff would be an awesome addition in L.A. and give them a new younger face of the franchise. Or this could a spot where a team like the Kings trade up to get the high scoring point guard.

name: Mikel Brown Jr.
scouting_text: Brown Jr. is a nuclear scorer who can light it up as well as anyone in this draft. He seems to be over the back issues that hindered him this season and his draft stock is pointing up. The Nets have plenty of guards but none that have the elite scoring upside like Brown Jr.

name: Kingston Flemings
scouting_text: – Kingston Flemings would be an awesome addition to the Kings. He’s more talented than anyone on their roster and is the type of dynamic athlete at the lead guard position that can change the course of a franchise. He didn’t measure as well as hoped for so that could knock him down a spot or two…but he shouldn’t fall too far!

name: Aday Mara
scouting_text: – Mara has been a steady riser all season and has been one of the stars of this week’s draft combine. He would be a nice addition upfront with his ability to anchor a defense and make plays on the offensive end. He’ll be one of the most fascinating players to watch as he adjusts to the NBA game, with his massive size and unique skill-set.

name: Keaton Wagler
pro_comp: taller Ryan Rollins, a hint of SGA, Haliburton
scouting_text: – Wagler would give the Mavs a versatile and highly talented big guard who has All Star upside. He’s been one of the most fascinating prospects to watch this season, rising from obscurity to a likely top 10 spot. He’d give the Mavs a stud young guard to grow next to Cooper and co.

name: Nate Ament
pro_comp: Risacher, Austin Croshere, Keith Van Horn
scouting_text: – Ament is a highly skilled offensive player with a nice outside shot but he has some limitations athletically and doesn’t really pressure the rim. He could be more of a role player at the next level, rather than a star.

name: Labaron Philon
scouting_text: – The Warriors are hoping they still have a small championship window open and will look to add a NBA-ready player who can provide meaningful minutes right away. Philon would be top 10 in many draft classes but in this strong class he falls to the Warriors at 11.

name: Brayden Burries
scouting_text: – The Thunder might look for a Wemby stopper but have enough assets to swing on another guard here. Burries is the type of all-around guard who can fit with any line-up. He would bring a great balance of scoring punch, two-way ability and the ability to play on or off the ball.

name: Karim Lopez
scouting_text: – One of the more underrated and unheralded players in the drat, Lopez has a chance to be really good as a do-it-all forward who contributes across the board.

name: Hannes Steinbach
scouting_text: –  I’m a massive Steinbach fan and think he’ll be really productive in the NBA. He’d be an awesomely unfair addition to the emerging Thunder dynasty.

name: Yaxel Lendeborg
scouting_text: – The Heat have a few solid pieces in place but are sort of stuck in no-man’s land. I’d love to see them swing for the fences with one of the younger, higher upside players in this draft but Yaxel seems like more of a Heat pick and would be solid value at this point in the draft.

name: Christian Anderson
scouting_text: So

name: Cameron Carr
scouting_text: – A long, lanky guard with a picture-perfect jumper and great above the rim ability. Needs to add strength and develop his frame but finally had his breakout season this year and is a first round lock. One of the best pure shooters in the draft class.

name: Chris Cenac Jr.
scouting_text: – A long and agile post player with a nice skill level and really good open court athleticism. Can face-up and take his man off the bounce or drain jumpers. Has great timing as a shot-blocker and can defend in space as well. Shows developing ball-handling and offensive play-making.

name: Morez Johnson
scouting_text: – A burly rebounding big man with good footwork in the paint. Gives good effort and his very productive on the boards and highly efficient with his scoring. Is starting to stretch the floor and show some upside on the offensive end. Probably won’t be a star in the NBA but seems to have a very clear path to success.

name: Jayden Quaintance
scouting_text: – JQ is an elite defensive talent with great strength, length, versatility and awesome defensive instincts. He’s a very pedestrian offensive player, limited to scoring around the rim, which limits his upside. He’s also been very slow to come back from his ACL injury and is now being held out of basketball activities. His injury history will certainly hinder his draft stock.

name: Ebuka Okorie
scouting_text: Fr

name: Henry Veesaar
scouting_text: Jr

name: Luigi Suigo
scouting_text: Int.07

name: Dailyn Swain
scouting_text: – A high level athlete with NBA level tools. Competes hard on both ends and has a lot of versatility. Struggles to shoot the ball from deep and has a lot of work to do with his offensive skill-set.

name: Koa Peat
scouting_text: – Peat is a rock solid player who has a NBA frame and does a lot of things well. He’s probably more of a role player than a first option/star player but I think he can be a very high-end role player. He doesn’t stretch the floor and has limitations on the offensive end but the 3pt shot will improve with time.

name: Bennett Stirtz
scouting_text: Sr

name: Zuby Ejiofor
scouting_text: – A tough, physical big man who lives in the paint and has developed a nice mid range jumper. A high energy player who’s arrow is pointing up.

name: Tarris Reed Jr.
scouting_text: – A burly big man who has really good footwork and is a difference maker in the paint. Measured out really well at the combine, helping his first round resume.
